careScope.stance: skill-coaching, reflection, planning, crisis redirection, and self-care support; not diagnosis or medical treatment
careScope.privacy: Local-first. The static version stores journal, mood, tasks, reminders, and safety-plan data in this browser's localStorage only.

crisisResources.primary: If there is immediate danger, call local emergency services now.
crisisResources.usCanada: In the U.S. and Canada, call or text 988 for the Suicide & Crisis Lifeline. You can also use 988 chat online.
safetySteps: Move away from anything you could use to harm yourself or someone else.
Get near another person, even if you cannot explain everything yet.
Use one short sentence: 'I am not safe alone right now. Please stay with me.'
Keep the chat open only as a bridge while you contact real-time help.

petulant_bpd_pattern.title: Petulant BPD support pattern
petulant_bpd_pattern.description: For rejection sensitivity, anger surges, protest behavior, jealousy, sudden mistrust, and the urge to punish or test connection.
petulant_bpd_pattern.validation: The alarm system is screaming that connection may be unsafe. We can protect the relationship without letting the alarm drive the car.

self_destructive_bpd_pattern.title: Self-destructive BPD support pattern
self_destructive_bpd_pattern.description: For self-punishment urges, self-harm urges, reckless impulses, substance urges, dissociation, or 'destroy my life' spirals.
self_destructive_bpd_pattern.validation: The urge makes sense as an emergency escape hatch, but you deserve a safer bridge through this peak.

discouraged_bpd_pattern.title: Discouraged BPD support pattern
discouraged_bpd_pattern.description: For collapse, shame, emptiness, hiding, self-blame, fawning, fear of being too much, and abandonment grief.
discouraged_bpd_pattern.validation: The shutdown is trying to prevent more pain. We can lower the demand and still keep you gently connected to care.

reactive_attachment_support.title: Reactive attachment / attachment-injury support
reactive_attachment_support.description: For trust panic, push-pull impulses, fear of closeness, caregiver wounds, testing, freezing, and difficulty believing safety is real.
reactive_attachment_support.validation: Part of you learned connection can be unpredictable. We can honor that history while checking what is true right now.

adhd_support.title: ADHD support
adhd_support.description: For executive dysfunction, time blindness, avoidance, working memory overload, stuck starts, and reminder loops.
adhd_support.validation: This is not laziness. Your brain needs smaller doors, less friction, and visible next steps.

autism_support.title: Autism support
autism_support.description: For sensory overload, shutdown, meltdown recovery, transitions, masking exhaustion, literal communication needs, and routine disruption.
autism_support.validation: Your nervous system may be overloaded, not 'overreacting.' We can reduce input and make the next step concrete.

anxiety_support.title: Anxiety support
anxiety_support.description: For panic, worry loops, racing thoughts, catastrophizing, rumination, and body alarm.
anxiety_support.validation: Your body is trying to protect you from a possible threat. We can thank the alarm and check the actual danger level.

crisisBridge.title: Crisis bridge
crisisBridge.when: Use when the person may harm themselves, cannot stay safe, or feels in immediate danger.
steps: Name it plainly: 'This is a safety moment, not a willpower test.'
Create distance from means: put the item in another room, give it to someone, or move yourself away.
Contact real-time help: emergency services, 988 in the U.S./Canada, or a trusted person nearby.
Use one body anchor while help is connecting: feet on floor, cold water, slow exhale.
crisisBridge.prompt: Are you able to move away from the means and contact a real person right now?

stop.title: STOP skill
steps: Stop. Do not send, spend, leave, accuse, or hurt yet.
Take a step back. Put the phone down or take one physical step away.
Observe: body sensations, thoughts, facts, urges, and what the relationship needs.
Proceed mindfully: choose the action that protects future-you.
stop.prompt: What urge are we pausing for the next 90 seconds?

tipp.title: TIPP skill
steps: Temperature: cold water on face, cold pack, or hold ice wrapped in cloth.
Intense movement: 30–60 seconds of safe movement if your body allows.
Paced breathing: longer exhale than inhale.
Paired muscle relaxation: tense gently, release slowly.
tipp.prompt: Pick the safest TIPP piece available to you right now.

urgeSurf.title: 10-minute urge surf
steps: Set a 10-minute timer.
Rate the urge 0–10; do not debate it.
Watch it as a wave: location in body, shape, heat, pressure.
When the timer ends, rerate and choose the next 10-minute bridge.
urgeSurf.prompt: What is the urge number right now?

grounding54321.title: 5-4-3-2-1 grounding
steps: Name 5 things you can see.
Name 4 things you can feel.
Name 3 things you can hear.
Name 2 things you can smell.
Name 1 thing you can taste or one safe sentence.
grounding54321.prompt: Start with 5 things you can see.

wiseMind.title: Wise Mind check-in
steps: Emotion Mind says: what feels urgent?
Reasonable Mind says: what are the facts?
Wise Mind says: what action fits both care and truth?
wiseMind.prompt: Give me one sentence from Emotion Mind and one from Reasonable Mind.

checkFacts.title: Check the Facts
steps: What happened, without mind-reading?
What story did my threat system add?
What evidence supports the story?
What evidence does not fit?
What is a balanced sentence I can act from?
checkFacts.prompt: What are the observable facts only?

oppositeAction.title: Opposite Action
steps: Name the emotion and urge.
Ask whether the emotion fits the facts and intensity.
If it does not fit, choose the opposite safe action.
Do it all the way for a small amount of time.
oppositeAction.prompt: What emotion is bossing you around right now?

please.title: PLEASE skill
steps: Treat physical illness or pain needs as real.
Balance eating as best you can.
Avoid mood-altering substances when possible.
Balance sleep and rest.
Move your body gently if available.
please.prompt: Which body need is loudest: food, water, meds, pain, rest, hygiene, movement?

dearMan.title: DEAR MAN request
steps: Describe facts briefly.
Express feelings without attack.
Assert the ask clearly.
Reinforce why it helps.
Mindful: stay on the ask.
Appear confident: short and steady.
Negotiate if needed.
dearMan.prompt: What is the actual ask, in one clear sentence?

giveFast.title: GIVE + FAST relationship balance
steps: GIVE: gentle, interested, validate, easy manner.
FAST: fair, no over-apologizing, stick to values, truthful.
Use both: kind to them and loyal to yourself.
giveFast.prompt: Which side is missing right now: kindness to them, or loyalty to you?

repairText.title: Repair text builder
steps: Lead with ownership of your behavior, not self-hatred.
Name the trigger without using it as an excuse.
State the repair action.
Ask for the next safe step.
repairText.template: I got flooded and reacted from fear. I’m not proud of how I handled it. I’m going to pause and regulate before we continue. When you can, can we reset and talk about what happened?
repairText.prompt: Do you need the soft version, firm boundary version, or apology version?

attachmentProtocol.title: Attachment storm protocol
steps: Name the attachment alarm: 'My body thinks connection is in danger.'
Separate past from present: 'This reminds me of then; I am checking now.'
Do one self-anchor before seeking reassurance.
Make one clear, non-testing request if contact is appropriate.
Respect the other person's timing while protecting your own safety.
attachmentProtocol.prompt: What did the alarm say they would do: leave, replace, punish, reject, or disappear?

adhdBridge.title: ADHD start bridge
steps: Make the task visible: write it in one verb phrase.
Shrink it until it feels almost silly.
Remove one friction point.
Start a 5-minute timer, not a life contract.
Stop or continue after the timer; both count as data.
adhdBridge.prompt: What task needs the tiniest possible doorway?

brainDumpPlan.title: Brain-dump to plan
steps: Dump everything without sorting.
Circle only what matters today.
Pick one body-care task, one admin task, one future-you task.
Break each into one 25-minute or 5-minute action.
brainDumpPlan.prompt: Paste the messy list; I’ll help split it.

pomodoro.title: Pomodoro flow
steps: Choose one task.
Work for 25 minutes.
Take a 5-minute break.
After four rounds, take a longer break.
pomodoro.prompt: Pick one task and start the timer in the tools panel.

sensoryReset.title: Sensory reset
steps: Lower input: lights, sound, texture, people, notifications.
Add regulation: pressure, warmth/cold, rocking, safe stim, or quiet.
Use concrete words: 'I need lower demand and less input.'
Delay non-urgent decisions until your body is less flooded.
sensoryReset.prompt: Which input is worst right now: sound, light, touch, smell, social demand, uncertainty?

lowDemandDay.title: Low-demand day protocol
steps: Keep only safety, meds, hydration, food, bathroom, and one tiny reset.
Swap 'should' for 'what is survivable?'
Use scripts instead of explaining: 'I am at low capacity today.'
Celebrate reduced harm as a valid win.
lowDemandDay.prompt: What is the smallest care task that would make the day 1% safer?

anxietyBreathing.title: Long-exhale breathing
steps: Inhale gently for 4.
Exhale slowly for 6 or 8.
Repeat 5 rounds.
Drop shoulders on each exhale.
Then name one next real-world step.
anxietyBreathing.prompt: Try one round now; what changed by even 1%?

worryContainer.title: Worry container
steps: Write every worry in fragments.
Mark each as now / later / not controllable.
Choose one 'now' action under 10 minutes.
Put the rest in a later container with a date or no-action label.
worryContainer.prompt: What worry keeps looping the loudest?

copeAhead.title: Cope Ahead
steps: Name the upcoming hard moment.
Predict the feeling and urge.
Choose the skill you will use first.
Rehearse the first 30 seconds in detail.
Add support and recovery afterward.
copeAhead.prompt: What moment are we preparing for?

selfCompassion.title: Anti-shame reset
steps: Use exact behavior language, not identity language.
Say: 'I did something I want to repair' instead of 'I am bad.'
Name one need underneath the behavior.
Choose one repair or care step.
selfCompassion.prompt: What are you calling yourself that we can translate into behavior language?

clearAsk.title: Clear ask script
steps: Say what you need literally.
Give a time or condition if possible.
Say what is optional vs required.
Avoid hidden tests; ask directly.
clearAsk.template: I’m overloaded and need a clear answer. Are you able to help with ___ by ___? If not, please tell me directly so I can plan another option.
clearAsk.prompt: What do you need stated directly?

transitionBridge.title: Transition bridge
steps: Name the current state and next state.
Create a visible countdown or cue.
Use a bridge object, playlist, drink, or body movement.
Start with only the first physical movement.

twoMinuteStart.title: Two-minute start
steps: Set a 2-minute timer.
Only prepare, open, gather, or touch the task.
Stop at 2 minutes if needed.
Give yourself credit for contact.
twoMinuteStart.prompt: What can you touch/open/gather in two minutes?

bodyDoubleScript.title: Body-double ask
steps: Ask for presence, not pressure.
State time box.
State task.
State how they can help.
bodyDoubleScript.template: Can you sit with me for 20 minutes while I start ___? You do not have to fix it; I just need company while I begin.
bodyDoubleScript.prompt: Who is a safe person to ask for quiet presence?

safetyPlan.title: Safety plan
steps: Warning signs
Internal coping steps
People and places for distraction
People to ask for help
Professional/emergency contacts
Means safety
Reasons to stay
safetyPlan.prompt: Use the Safety Plan panel to fill this in while you are calm enough.

dbtSkillFinder.title: DBT workbook skill finder
dbtSkillFinder.when: Use when you know the problem, emotion, urge, or relationship situation but not which DBT skill or worksheet fits.
steps: Name the situation in one sentence.
Pick the module that sounds closest: mindfulness, interpersonal effectiveness, emotion regulation, distress tolerance, or behavior analysis.
Search the DBT library panel with the emotion, urge, acronym, or skill name.
Open a handout for coaching or a worksheet form for practice.
dbtSkillFinder.prompt: What emotion, urge, relationship problem, or body state should the DBT library search for?

chainAnalysis.title: Chain analysis bridge
chainAnalysis.when: Use after an ineffective behavior or a near-miss to understand causes without shame.
steps: Describe the target behavior specifically.
Name the prompting event that began the chain.
List vulnerability factors that made the chain easier to start.
Trace links: thoughts, feelings, body sensations, actions, events, and urges.
Notice consequences, then choose skillful replacement links and repair steps.
chainAnalysis.prompt: What behavior or near-miss are we analyzing without blame?

missingLinksAnalysis.title: Missing-links analysis bridge
missingLinksAnalysis.when: Use when an effective behavior did not happen, even though you wanted, planned, or agreed to do it.
steps: Name the behavior that was needed.
Check whether you knew it was needed, remembered it, and understood how to do it.
Identify what got in the way: emotion, avoidance, environment, skills gap, or willfulness.
Problem-solve the missing link for next time.
missingLinksAnalysis.prompt: What needed behavior did not happen?

radicalAcceptance.title: Radical acceptance bridge
radicalAcceptance.when: Use when pain is being amplified by fighting reality, replaying unfairness, or refusing what already is.
steps: Name the reality you are practicing accepting, without approving it.
Notice body resistance: tension, no, why me, this cannot be true.
Turn the mind toward willingness again and again.
Choose one action that fits reality as it is right now.
radicalAcceptance.prompt: What reality are you trying to accept without saying it is okay?

prosConsDbt.title: DBT pros and cons bridge
prosConsDbt.when: Use when urges are strong and you need Wise Mind to compare acting on the urge versus using skills.
steps: Write the urge or choice.
List short-term pros and cons of acting on the urge.
List long-term pros and cons of acting on the urge.
List short-term and long-term effects of using skills instead.
Read the long-term column before choosing.
prosConsDbt.prompt: What urge or choice needs a pros-and-cons check?

improveMoment.title: IMPROVE the moment bridge
improveMoment.when: Use when the situation cannot be solved immediately but the moment can be made more survivable.
steps: Imagine a safe enough place or outcome.
Find one tiny meaning, prayer, grounding phrase, or value.
Relax one part of the body.
Do one thing at a time.
Take a brief vacation from the problem if safe.
Encourage yourself with a kind, believable sentence.
improveMoment.prompt: Which IMPROVE piece is available in this moment?

quickActions.label: Unsafe urge
quickActions.message: I might not be safe or I have an unsafe urge.

quickActions.label: Attachment panic
quickActions.message: I am having an attachment panic and I think they will leave.

quickActions.label: Anger / rejection spike
quickActions.message: I feel rejected and angry and I want to react.

quickActions.label: Shame collapse
quickActions.message: I feel like a burden and I want to disappear.

quickActions.label: Sensory overload
quickActions.message: I am overstimulated and need a sensory reset.

quickActions.label: Relationship script
quickActions.message: Help me ask for what I need without escalating.

quickActions.label: DBT skill finder
quickActions.message: Help me find the right DBT skill or worksheet for this.

quickActions.label: Chain analysis
quickActions.message: Help me do a chain analysis without shame.

quickActions.label: Check the Facts
quickActions.message: Help me check the facts before I react.

quickActions.label: Opposite Action
quickActions.message: Help me use opposite action for this emotion.

quickActions.label: Pros & Cons
quickActions.message: Help me do DBT pros and cons for this urge.

starterMessages: Hi momma. I’m here for skills, grounding, planning, self-care, and safer next steps. I can help with BPD-pattern spirals, attachment alarms, ADHD task paralysis, autistic overload, and anxiety. I’m not a therapist or crisis service, but I can help you bridge to care.
Tell me what is happening in plain messy words, or tap a quick action. We’ll make it smaller and safer.

used: Telegram reminder concepts converted to local browser reminders
DBT modules: mindfulness, emotion regulation, distress tolerance, interpersonal effectiveness
interactive flow concept during emotional overload
not merged into wellness logic because these are data-build-tool/devops repositories, not dialectical behavior therapy chatbots

sourceMerge.source: DBT JSON catalogs from uploaded workbook

dbtIntegration.copyright_note: The embedded DBT material is a paraphrased metadata and worksheet-schema catalog, not reproduced full workbook text.
